questions.question: What is the primary reason thread-per-connection servers (like the one in Lesson 10) fail to scale beyond ~10,000 concurrent connections?
options: TCP's maximum concurrent connection limit is 10,000
Each OS thread allocates a large fixed stack (~2 MB), consuming terabytes of memory at scale
The kernel limits the total number of file descriptors to 10,024 per process
Threads cannot share the same listening socket
questions.explanation: The C10K problem: each OS thread reserves a fixed stack (~2 MB on Linux). At 1 million connections, that's 2 TB of virtual memory just for stacks, most of it unused. The kernel scheduler also degrades badly with that many threads. Async I/O solves this by driving many connections from a small pool of threads, where per-connection state is a few hundred bytes (not 2 MB).

questions.question: What kernel mechanism do production servers (nginx, Redis) use to monitor thousands of sockets from a single thread?
options: select() with a large fd_set
poll() with a large pollfd array
epoll on Linux / kqueue on macOS
SIGIO signal-driven I/O
questions.explanation: epoll (Linux) and kqueue (macOS/BSD) scale to thousands of file descriptors with O(1) registration and event collection. select() and poll() scan all descriptors linearly on every call (O(n)), making them unusable beyond a few hundred connections. epoll/kqueue notify the application only when a socket becomes ready, without scanning.

questions.question: What happens at the OS level when a Tokio task calls socket.read(&mut buf).await and no data has arrived yet?
options: The OS blocks the thread until data arrives, then resumes the task
The runtime registers the socket with epoll for EPOLLIN, returns Poll::Pending, and the thread goes on to poll other tasks
The runtime busy-loops polling the socket until data arrives
The task is immediately cancelled and must be re-spawned when data arrives
questions.explanation: When a future returns Poll::Pending, the runtime deschedules the task and polls another one. The I/O driver has registered the socket fd with epoll. When the kernel detects data on the socket, it generates an epoll event. The I/O driver receives this event, calls the associated Waker, which tells the scheduler to re-queue the task. Then the runtime polls the read future again, and this time data is available so it returns Poll::Ready(n). No thread was blocked during this entire flow.

questions.question: What is the role of the Waker in Rust's async cooperative scheduling model?
options: It prevents other tasks from running while a future is being polled
It tells the executor which thread should poll a given future
It is a callback that signals to the executor that a future should be polled again because it can now make progress
It measures how long a future takes to complete for performance monitoring
questions.explanation: The Waker is created by the executor and passed to every Future via Context. When a future returns Poll::Pending (e.g., waiting for data on a socket), it stores a clone of the Waker. When the I/O event arrives (the socket becomes readable), the I/O driver calls waker.wake(), which tells the executor: 'this task is ready, poll it again.' This is the core of cooperative scheduling — futures tell the executor when they're ready to make progress, rather than being polled in a busy loop.

questions.question: Why must CPU-bound computation inside a Tokio async function be wrapped in tokio::task::spawn_blocking?
options: Because the Rust compiler refuses to compile CPU-bound code inside async functions
Because async functions cannot call regular function calls
Because long-running CPU work blocks the worker thread, preventing it from polling other tasks and causing starvation
Because CPU-bound work always panics when run inside a tokio runtime
questions.explanation: Tokio uses cooperative scheduling — tasks only yield at .await points. If you run a CPU-intensive loop without any .await calls inside an async fn, the worker thread servicing that task cannot poll any other tasks. This starves all other tasks on that thread. spawn_blocking moves the work to a separate thread pool dedicated to blocking/CPU work, keeping the async worker threads free to poll I/O-bound tasks.
